agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal), centralizing cross-cutting concerns while maintaining strict traffic isolation. This eliminates duplicated auth logic across services, enforces consistent rate limiting, and creates a clear boundary between external and internal traffic.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic. Terminates TLS, performs initial request validation (header checks, payload size limits), routes to the correct authentication pipeline based on ingress path, and applies rate limiting. Does NOT contain business logic.","boundaries":"Handles only cross-cutting concerns: auth, rate limiting, request routing, observability headers (correlation IDs, tracing). Never calls databases directly — delegates auth verification to the Auth Service."},{"name":"Mobile Ingress Pipeline","responsibility":"Processes customer-facing mobile app requests. Validates JWT/OAuth2 bearer tokens via the Auth Service, enforces 100 req/min per authenticated user ID, transforms requests to internal format, and routes to downstream services.","boundaries":"Only accepts traffic on the public mobile endpoint. Rate limit keyed on user ID extracted from token claims."},{"name":"Partner Ingress Pipeline","responsibility":"Processes B2B partner integration requests. Validates API keys via the Auth Service, enforces 1,000 req/min per partner ID, manages webhook registration and delivery, and routes to downstream services.","boundaries":"Only accepts traffic on the partner endpoint. Rate limit keyed on partner ID from API key lookup. Webhook delivery is async and decoupled from request processing."},{"name":"Internal Service Mesh","responsibility":"Handles service-to-service communication with no authentication overhead. Provides service discovery, load balancing, circuit breaking, and mutual TLS for transport security (not identity auth).","boundaries":"Completely isolated from external traffic at the network level (separate VPC/network segment, no public ingress). Trust is established by network boundary, not credentials."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Centralized authentication and authorization. Validates JWT tokens (mobile), API keys (partner), and manages token issuance/refresh. Single source of truth for auth logic — gateway delegates all verification here.","boundaries":"Stateless verification with cached key material. Owns the user session and partner credential stores. Does not make authorization decisions beyond identity verification — authorization is the downstream service's responsibility."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Centralized rate limiting using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Exposes a simple check-and-decrement interface. Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) for gateway to include in responses.","boundaries":"Shared Redis cluster for distributed counting. Keyed on (consumer_type, consumer_id). Gateway calls this synchronously on every request — must have sub-millisecond p99 latency."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages webhook subscriptions for partners, reliably delivers events with retry logic (exponential backoff, max 5 retries over 24h), and tracks delivery status. Partners can query delivery status via the partner API.","boundaries":"Consumes domain events from an event bus. Owns the webhook subscription registry and delivery log. Isolated from the request path — never blocks API responses."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS requests to api.platform.com/mobile/v1/* with Authorization: Bearer <JWT> header","coupling_type":"loose — versioned REST API with standard OAuth2 token format"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS requests to api.platform.com/partner/v1/* with X-API-Key header","coupling_type":"loose — versioned REST API with opaque API key"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"gRPC call: VerifyToken(token, token_type) -> (identity, claims, valid) and VerifyAPIKey(key) -> (partner_id, scopes, valid)","coupling_type":"tight — synchronous on every request, must be highly available"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"gRPC call: CheckRate(consumer_type, consumer_id, limit) -> (allowed, remaining, reset_at)","coupling_type":"tight — synchronous on every request, must be sub-ms latency"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Downstream Services","contract":"HTTP/gRPC with enriched headers: X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-ID, X-Correlation-ID. Downstream services MUST NOT accept these headers from external sources.","coupling_type":"loose — gateway strips and re-injects trusted headers"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Services","contract":"gRPC/HTTP over service mesh with mTLS. Service discovery via DNS or registry. No gateway hop.","coupling_type":"medium — shared service mesh infrastructure but independent deployment"},{"from":"Downstream Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to event bus (e.g., order.shipped, delivery.completed). Dispatcher subscribes and matches to partner webhook registrations.","coupling_type":"loose — async event-driven, no direct coupling"}],"data_flow":"External request → TLS termination at gateway → path-based routing to mobile or partner pipeline → Auth Service verification (JWT or API key) → Rate Limiter check → header enrichment with verified identity → route to downstream service → downstream processes request using trusted identity headers → response flows back through gateway → rate limit headers injected → response to client. For webhooks: downstream service emits domain event → event bus → Webhook Dispatcher matches subscriptions → HTTP POST to partner endpoint with HMAC signature → retry on failure.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend (BFF)","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns that were inconsistently implemented across services. Separate ingress pipelines per consumer type act as lightweight BFFs, each tailored to its consumer's auth model and rate limits without a monolithic gateway config."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (Migration)","rationale":"Services currently handle their own auth. Rather than a big-bang migration, route one service at a time through the gateway, verify behavior, then remove per-service auth. This de-risks the transition."},{"pattern":"Sidecar / Service Mesh for Internal Traffic","rationale":"Internal services need isolation from external traffic, not authentication. A service mesh provides transport security (mTLS) and observability without the overhead of credential management. Network-level isolation is the primary security boundary."},{"pattern":"Token Relay with Trusted Headers","rationale":"Gateway verifies identity once and passes trusted claims via headers to downstream services. Downstream services never re-validate tokens — they trust the gateway. This eliminates duplicated auth logic while keeping services stateless."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed-window counters (avoids burst-at-boundary problem) while simpler than token bucket for this scale. Redis-backed for distributed consistency across gateway instances."},{"pattern":"Async Event-Driven Webhooks","rationale":"Decouples webhook delivery from the request path. Partner webhook reliability (retries, backoff) is complex — isolating it in a dedicated dispatcher prevents it from affecting API latency or reliability."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"API Access Control","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["ConsumerIdentity (mobile user or partner)","RateLimitPolicy","APIKeyCredential"]},{"name":"Webhook Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription","DeliveryAttempt"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Delivery — existing domain, untouched by gateway design"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Logistics Core publishes domain events, Webhook Management subscribes. Core is unaware of webhooks."},{"upstream":"API Access Control","downstream":"Logistics Core","relationship":"Open Host Service — Gateway presents a clean, versioned API surface. Core services consume trusted identity headers without coupling to auth implementation."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single logical gateway with separate ingress pipelines (not three separate gateways)","gains":"Shared infrastructure, consistent observability, single deployment artifact, easier ops","sacrifices":"Blast radius — a gateway bug affects all consumer types. Slightly more complex routing config.","alternatives_considered":["Separate gateway per consumer type — better isolation but 3x operational overhead and config drift risk","Service mesh ingress only — insufficient for the auth centralization requirement"]},{"decision":"Centralized Auth Service rather than gateway-embedded auth","gains":"Auth logic in one place, testable independently, can evolve auth schemes without redeploying gateway, reusable by other components","sacrifices":"Additional network hop on every request (mitigated by gRPC + connection pooling + local caching of public keys)","alternatives_considered":["Embed auth logic in gateway plugins — faster but scatters auth logic and couples it to gateway vendor","Per-service auth (status quo) — rejected because it is the problem being solved"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed distributed rate limiter rather than local per-instance counters","gains":"Accurate global rate limiting across all gateway instances, consistent enforcement","sacrifices":"Redis dependency on the critical path — if Redis is down, must decide: fail-open (risk abuse) or fail-closed (risk availability)","alternatives_considered":["Local in-memory counters — no external dependency but inaccurate with multiple gateway instances","Token bucket with local + periodic sync — complex implementation for marginal accuracy gain"]},{"decision":"Strangler fig migration rather than big-bang cutover","gains":"Low risk, incremental validation, ability to rollback per-service","sacrifices":"Temporary period where some services use gateway auth and others use per-service auth — operational complexity during migration","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang migration — faster but high risk; any gateway bug blocks all services"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Auth mechanisms","evolution_path":"Auth Service is behind a clean interface. Can add SSO, OAuth2 scopes, mTLS client certs, or OIDC federation without changing gateway routing logic."},{"area":"Rate limiting policies","evolution_path":"Rate limits are configuration-driven per consumer type and ID. Can add tiered rate limits, burst allowances, or per-endpoint limits by changing config, not code."},{"area":"New consumer types","evolution_path":"Adding a new ingress pipeline (e.g., public API for third-party developers) requires a new route prefix and auth config — no changes to downstream services."},{"area":"Gateway technology","evolution_path":"Gateway is a routing + plugin layer. Can start with Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway and migrate later. Downstream services only see trusted headers, not gateway internals."},{"area":"Webhook delivery","evolution_path":"Dispatcher is fully decoupled. Can add filtering, transformation, or fan-out without affecting the core API path."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Trusted header contract","reason":"All downstream services must trust X-Consumer-Type and X-Consumer-ID headers from the gateway and reject these headers from any other source. Changing this contract requires coordinated updates across all services."},{"area":"Network isolation boundary","reason":"Internal services MUST NOT be reachable from external networks. This is a security invariant enforced at the infrastructure level. Violating this defeats the entire internal traffic isolation design."},{"area":"Rate limit key structure","reason":"Rate limits are keyed on (consumer_type, consumer_id). Changing this key structure requires Redis migration and careful coordination to avoid windows of unenforced limits."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes a single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway as a horizontally scaled, stateless cluster behind a load balancer. Health checks with automatic instance replacement. Circuit breaker to Auth Service and Rate Limiter with fail-open policy (log and allow) for brief outages."},{"risk":"Auth Service latency spikes add directly to every request's latency","severity":"high","mitigation":"Cache JWT public keys locally in gateway (refresh every 60s). Cache API key lookups with short TTL (30s). Use gRPC with persistent connections. Set strict timeout (50ms) with circuit breaker fallback."},{"risk":"Redis rate limiter failure causes either abuse (fail-open) or outage (fail-closed)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Default to fail-open with alerting. Use Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Fall back to local approximate counters during Redis outages — imprecise but prevents both extremes."},{"risk":"Strangler fig migration creates a long period of inconsistent auth enforcement","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Prioritize migration by risk: highest-traffic and most security-sensitive services first. Set a hard deadline (e.g., 8 weeks) to complete migration. Automated tests verify both auth paths during transition."},{"risk":"Partner webhook delivery failures create support burden and data inconsistency","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Exponential backoff with 5 retries over 24h. Dead-letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Partner-facing dashboard showing delivery status. Alert on sustained failure rates per partner."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-2): Deploy API gateway (recommend Kong or Envoy-based) with TLS termination and path-based routing. No auth — just pass-through to prove routing works. Phase 2 (Weeks 3-4): Deploy Auth Service and Rate Limiter. Wire gateway to call them. Validate with synthetic traffic. Phase 3 (Weeks 5-8): Strangler fig migration — route services through gateway one at a time, starting with the least critical. Remove per-service auth as each service is verified. Phase 4 (Weeks 9-10): Deploy Webhook Dispatcher for partner integrations. Phase 5 (Ongoing): Remove legacy per-service auth code, harden monitoring, tune rate limits based on real traffic patterns.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/api-gateway/ — Gateway configuration (routes, plugins, rate limit policies)","services/auth-service/ — New centralized auth service (JWT verification, API key lookup)","services/rate-limiter/ — Rate limiting service with Redis backend","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — Webhook subscription management and delivery","libs/gateway-headers/ — Shared library defining trusted header constants and validation helpers","infra/network/ — Network policies enforcing internal/external traffic isolation","docs/adr/001-api-gateway-architecture.md — Architecture Decision Record for this design"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use gateway plugins for cross-cutting concerns, never business logic. Downstream services must validate that trusted headers are present but never re-authenticate. Rate limit responses must include standard headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset). All gateway errors return RFC 7807 Problem Details format. Webhook payloads signed with HMAC-SHA256 using per-partner secrets.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it must remain a thin routing and policy enforcement layer","Do NOT allow downstream services to accept X-Consumer-* headers from sources other than the gateway — this is a privilege escalation vector","Do NOT use a single rate limit bucket for all endpoints — some endpoints (e.g., search) need tighter limits than others","Do NOT skip the strangler fig approach — big-bang gateway cutover is the #1 cause of gateway migration failures","Do NOT store API keys in plaintext — hash them; the Auth Service should compare hashes","Do NOT make webhook delivery synchronous — it will destroy API latency and couple availability to partner endpoint health"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-pipeline ingress design — clearly driven by the three distinct consumer types with different auth and rate limit needs","Centralized auth over per-service auth — directly solves the stated inconsistency problem","Network isolation for internal services — standard practice, well-understood","Strangler fig migration — proven pattern for gateway adoption"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-native) — depends on team expertise, existing infrastructure, and cloud provider which are unknown","Redis sizing and failover strategy — depends on actual traffic volumes which are not specified","Webhook delivery SLA expectations — no requirements stated for delivery latency or retry policy"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/network segmentation capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate (the stated rate limits suggest thousands of req/min, not millions)","The team has operational capacity to run a gateway cluster and Redis","JWT is acceptable for mobile auth and opaque API keys for partners — no specific auth standard was mandated","Services communicate via HTTP or gRPC internally"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal), centralizing cross-cutting concerns while maintaining strict traffic isolation. This eliminates duplicated auth logic across services, enforces consistent rate limiting, and creates a clear boundary between external and internal traffic.","architectur

the Auth Service should compare hashes","Do NOT make webhook delivery synchronous — it will destroy API latency and couple availability to partner endpoint health"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-pipeline ingress design — clearly driven by the three distinct consumer types with different auth and rate limit needs","Centralized auth over per-service auth — directly solves the stated inconsistency problem","Network isolation for internal services — standard practice, well-understood","Strangler fig migration — proven pattern for gateway adoption"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-native) — depends on team expertise, existing infrastructure, and cloud provider which are unknown","Redis sizing and failover strategy — depends on actual traffic volumes which are not specified","Webhook delivery SLA expectations — no requirements stated for delivery latency or retry policy"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/network segmentation capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate (the stated rate limits suggest thousands of req/min, not millions)","The team has operational capacity to run a gateway cluster and Redis","JWT is acceptable for mobile auth and opaque API keys for partners — no specific auth standard was mandated","Services communicate via HTTP or gRPC internally"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal), centralizing cross-cutting concerns while maintaining strict traffic isolation. This eliminates duplicated auth logic across services, enforces consistent rate limiting, and creates a clear boundary between external and internal traffic.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway (Edge)","responsibility":"Single entry point for all external traffic. Terminates TLS, performs initial request validation (header checks, payload size limits), routes to the correct authentication pipeline based on ingress path, and applies rate limiting. Does NOT contain business logic.","boundaries":"Handles only cross-cutting concerns: auth, rate limiting, request routing, observability headers (correlation IDs, tracing). Never calls databases directly — delegates auth verification to the Auth Service."},{"name":"Mobile Ingress Pipeline","responsibility":"Processes customer-facing mobile app requests. Validates JWT/OAuth2 bearer tokens via the Auth Service, enforces 100 req/min per authenticated user ID, transforms requests to internal format, and routes to downstream services.","boundaries":"Only accepts traffic on the public mobile endpoint. Rate limit keyed on user ID extracted from token claims."},{"name":"Partner Ingress Pipeline","responsibility":"Processes B2B partner integration requests. Validates API keys via the Auth Service, enforces 1,000 req/min per partner ID, manages webhook registration and delivery, and routes to downstream services.","boundaries":"Only accepts traffic on the partner endpoint. Rate limit keyed on partner ID from API key lookup. Webhook delivery is async and decoupled from request processing."},{"name":"Internal Service Mesh","responsibility":"Handles service-to-service communication with no authentication overhead. Provides service discovery, load balancing, circuit breaking, and mutual TLS for transport security (not identity auth).","boundaries":"Completely isolated from external traffic at the network level (separate VPC/network segment, no public ingress). Trust is established by network boundary, not credentials."},{"name":"Auth Service","responsibility":"Centralized authentication and authorization. Validates JWT tokens (mobile), API keys (partner), and manages token issuance/refresh. Single source of truth for auth logic — gateway delegates all verification here.","boundaries":"Stateless verification with cached key material. Owns the user session and partner credential stores. Does not make authorization decisions beyond identity verification — authorization is the downstream service's responsibility."},{"name":"Rate Limiter","responsibility":"Centralized rate limiting using a sliding window algorithm backed by Redis. Exposes a simple check-and-decrement interface. Returns rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) for gateway to include in responses.","boundaries":"Shared Redis cluster for distributed counting. Keyed on (consumer_type, consumer_id). Gateway calls this synchronously on every request — must have sub-millisecond p99 latency."},{"name":"Webhook Dispatcher","responsibility":"Manages webhook subscriptions for partners, reliably delivers events with retry logic (exponential backoff, max 5 retries over 24h), and tracks delivery status. Partners can query delivery status via the partner API.","boundaries":"Consumes domain events from an event bus. Owns the webhook subscription registry and delivery log. Isolated from the request path — never blocks API responses."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Mobile App","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS requests to api.platform.com/mobile/v1/* with Authorization: Bearer <JWT> header","coupling_type":"loose — versioned REST API with standard OAuth2 token format"},{"from":"Partner Systems","to":"API Gateway (Edge)","contract":"HTTPS requests to api.platform.com/partner/v1/* with X-API-Key header","coupling_type":"loose — versioned REST API with opaque API key"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Auth Service","contract":"gRPC call: VerifyToken(token, token_type) -> (identity, claims, valid) and VerifyAPIKey(key) -> (partner_id, scopes, valid)","coupling_type":"tight — synchronous on every request, must be highly available"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Rate Limiter","contract":"gRPC call: CheckRate(consumer_type, consumer_id, limit) -> (allowed, remaining, reset_at)","coupling_type":"tight — synchronous on every request, must be sub-ms latency"},{"from":"API Gateway (Edge)","to":"Downstream Services","contract":"HTTP/gRPC with enriched headers: X-Consumer-Type, X-Consumer-ID, X-Correlation-ID. Downstream services MUST NOT accept these headers from external sources.","coupling_type":"loose — gateway strips and re-injects trusted headers"},{"from":"Internal Services","to":"Internal Services","contract":"gRPC/HTTP over service mesh with mTLS. Service discovery via DNS or registry. No gateway hop.","coupling_type":"medium — shared service mesh infrastructure but independent deployment"},{"from":"Downstream Services","to":"Webhook Dispatcher","contract":"Domain events published to event bus (e.g., order.shipped, delivery.completed). Dispatcher subscribes and matches to partner webhook registrations.","coupling_type":"loose — async event-driven, no direct coupling"}],"data_flow":"External request → TLS termination at gateway → path-based routing to mobile or partner pipeline → Auth Service verification (JWT or API key) → Rate Limiter check → header enrichment with verified identity → route to downstream service → downstream processes request using trusted identity headers → response flows back through gateway → rate limit headers injected → response to client. For webhooks: downstream service emits domain event → event bus → Webhook Dispatcher matches subscriptions → HTTP POST to partner endpoint with HMAC signature → retry on failure.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"API Gateway / Backend for Frontend (BFF)","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns that were inconsistently implemented across services. Separate ingress pipelines per consumer type act as lightweight BFFs, each tailored to its consumer's auth model and rate limits without a monolithic gateway config."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (Migration)","rationale":"Services currently handle their own auth. Rather than a big-bang migration, route one service at a time through the gateway, verify behavior, then remove per-service auth. This de-risks the transition."},{"pattern":"Sidecar / Service Mesh for Internal Traffic","rationale":"Internal services need isolation from external traffic, not authentication. A service mesh provides transport security (mTLS) and observability without the overhead of credential management. Network-level isolation is the primary security boundary."},{"pattern":"Token Relay with Trusted Headers","rationale":"Gateway verifies identity once and passes trusted claims via headers to downstream services. Downstream services never re-validate tokens — they trust the gateway. This eliminates duplicated auth logic while keeping services stateless."},{"pattern":"Sliding Window Rate Limiting","rationale":"More accurate than fixed-window counters (avoids burst-at-boundary problem) while simpler than token bucket for this scale. Redis-backed for distributed consistency across gateway instances."},{"pattern":"Async Event-Driven Webhooks","rationale":"Decouples webhook delivery from the request path. Partner webhook reliability (retries, backoff) is complex — isolating it in a dedicated dispatcher prevents it from affecting API latency or reliability."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"API Access Control","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["ConsumerIdentity (mobile user or partner)","RateLimitPolicy","APIKeyCredential"]},{"name":"Webhook Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["WebhookSubscription","DeliveryAttempt"]},{"name":"Logistics Core","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","Shipment","Delivery — existing domain, untouched by gateway design"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Logistics Core","downstream":"Webhook Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Logistics Core publishes domain events, Webhook Management subscribes. Core is unaware of webhooks."},{"upstream":"API Access Control","downstream":"Logistics Core","relationship":"Open Host Service — Gateway presents a clean, versioned API surface. Core services consume trusted identity headers without coupling to auth implementation."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Single logical gateway with separate ingress pipelines (not three separate gateways)","gains":"Shared infrastructure, consistent observability, single deployment artifact, easier ops","sacrifices":"Blast radius — a gateway bug affects all consumer types. Slightly more complex routing config.","alternatives_considered":["Separate gateway per consumer type — better isolation but 3x operational overhead and config drift risk","Service mesh ingress only — insufficient for the auth centralization requirement"]},{"decision":"Centralized Auth Service rather than gateway-embedded auth","gains":"Auth logic in one place, testable independently, can evolve auth schemes without redeploying gateway, reusable by other components","sacrifices":"Additional network hop on every request (mitigated by gRPC + connection pooling + local caching of public keys)","alternatives_considered":["Embed auth logic in gateway plugins — faster but scatters auth logic and couples it to gateway vendor","Per-service auth (status quo) — rejected because it is the problem being solved"]},{"decision":"Redis-backed distributed rate limiter rather than local per-instance counters","gains":"Accurate global rate limiting across all gateway instances, consistent enforcement","sacrifices":"Redis dependency on the critical path — if Redis is down, must decide: fail-open (risk abuse) or fail-closed (risk availability)","alternatives_considered":["Local in-memory counters — no external dependency but inaccurate with multiple gateway instances","Token bucket with local + periodic sync — complex implementation for marginal accuracy gain"]},{"decision":"Strangler fig migration rather than big-bang cutover","gains":"Low risk, incremental validation, ability to rollback per-service","sacrifices":"Temporary period where some services use gateway auth and others use per-service auth — operational complexity during migration","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang migration — faster but high risk; any gateway bug blocks all services"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Auth mechanisms","evolution_path":"Auth Service is behind a clean interface. Can add SSO, OAuth2 scopes, mTLS client certs, or OIDC federation without changing gateway routing logic."},{"area":"Rate limiting policies","evolution_path":"Rate limits are configuration-driven per consumer type and ID. Can add tiered rate limits, burst allowances, or per-endpoint limits by changing config, not code."},{"area":"New consumer types","evolution_path":"Adding a new ingress pipeline (e.g., public API for third-party developers) requires a new route prefix and auth config — no changes to downstream services."},{"area":"Gateway technology","evolution_path":"Gateway is a routing + plugin layer. Can start with Kong/Envoy/AWS API Gateway and migrate later. Downstream services only see trusted headers, not gateway internals."},{"area":"Webhook delivery","evolution_path":"Dispatcher is fully decoupled. Can add filtering, transformation, or fan-out without affecting the core API path."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Trusted header contract","reason":"All downstream services must trust X-Consumer-Type and X-Consumer-ID headers from the gateway and reject these headers from any other source. Changing this contract requires coordinated updates across all services."},{"area":"Network isolation boundary","reason":"Internal services MUST NOT be reachable from external networks. This is a security invariant enforced at the infrastructure level. Violating this defeats the entire internal traffic isolation design."},{"area":"Rate limit key structure","reason":"Rate limits are keyed on (consumer_type, consumer_id). Changing this key structure requires Redis migration and careful coordination to avoid windows of unenforced limits."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Gateway becomes a single point of failure for all external traffic","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy gateway as a horizontally scaled, stateless cluster behind a load balancer. Health checks with automatic instance replacement. Circuit breaker to Auth Service and Rate Limiter with fail-open policy (log and allow) for brief outages."},{"risk":"Auth Service latency spikes add directly to every request's latency","severity":"high","mitigation":"Cache JWT public keys locally in gateway (refresh every 60s). Cache API key lookups with short TTL (30s). Use gRPC with persistent connections. Set strict timeout (50ms) with circuit breaker fallback."},{"risk":"Redis rate limiter failure causes either abuse (fail-open) or outage (fail-closed)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Default to fail-open with alerting. Use Redis Sentinel or Cluster for HA. Fall back to local approximate counters during Redis outages — imprecise but prevents both extremes."},{"risk":"Strangler fig migration creates a long period of inconsistent auth enforcement","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Prioritize migration by risk: highest-traffic and most security-sensitive services first. Set a hard deadline (e.g., 8 weeks) to complete migration. Automated tests verify both auth paths during transition."},{"risk":"Partner webhook delivery failures create support burden and data inconsistency","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Exponential backoff with 5 retries over 24h. Dead-letter queue for permanently failed deliveries. Partner-facing dashboard showing delivery status. Alert on sustained failure rates per partner."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (Weeks 1-2): Deploy API gateway (recommend Kong or Envoy-based) with TLS termination and path-based routing. No auth — just pass-through to prove routing works. Phase 2 (Weeks 3-4): Deploy Auth Service and Rate Limiter. Wire gateway to call them. Validate with synthetic traffic. Phase 3 (Weeks 5-8): Strangler fig migration — route services through gateway one at a time, starting with the least critical. Remove per-service auth as each service is verified. Phase 4 (Weeks 9-10): Deploy Webhook Dispatcher for partner integrations. Phase 5 (Ongoing): Remove legacy per-service auth code, harden monitoring, tune rate limits based on real traffic patterns.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infra/api-gateway/ — Gateway configuration (routes, plugins, rate limit policies)","services/auth-service/ — New centralized auth service (JWT verification, API key lookup)","services/rate-limiter/ — Rate limiting service with Redis backend","services/webhook-dispatcher/ — Webhook subscription management and delivery","libs/gateway-headers/ — Shared library defining trusted header constants and validation helpers","infra/network/ — Network policies enforcing internal/external traffic isolation","docs/adr/001-api-gateway-architecture.md — Architecture Decision Record for this design"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use gateway plugins for cross-cutting concerns, never business logic. Downstream services must validate that trusted headers are present but never re-authenticate. Rate limit responses must include standard headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset). All gateway errors return RFC 7807 Problem Details format. Webhook payloads signed with HMAC-SHA256 using per-partner secrets.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in the gateway — it must remain a thin routing and policy enforcement layer","Do NOT allow downstream services to accept X-Consumer-* headers from sources other than the gateway — this is a privilege escalation vector","Do NOT use a single rate limit bucket for all endpoints — some endpoints (e.g., search) need tighter limits than others","Do NOT skip the strangler fig approach — big-bang gateway cutover is the #1 cause of gateway migration failures","Do NOT store API keys in plaintext — hash them; the Auth Service should compare hashes","Do NOT make webhook delivery synchronous — it will destroy API latency and couple availability to partner endpoint health"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Three-pipeline ingress design — clearly driven by the three distinct consumer types with different auth and rate limit needs","Centralized auth over per-service auth — directly solves the stated inconsistency problem","Network isolation for internal services — standard practice, well-understood","Strangler fig migration — proven pattern for gateway adoption"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific gateway technology choice (Kong vs Envoy vs cloud-native) — depends on team expertise, existing infrastructure, and cloud provider which are unknown","Redis sizing and failover strategy — depends on actual traffic volumes which are not specified","Webhook delivery SLA expectations — no requirements stated for delivery latency or retry policy"],"assumptions":["The platform runs on cloud infrastructure with VPC/network segmentation capabilities","Traffic volume is moderate (the stated rate limits suggest thousands of req/min, not millions)","The team has operational capacity to run a gateway cluster and Redis","JWT is acceptable for mobile auth and opaque API keys for partners — no specific auth standard was mandated","Services communicate via HTTP or gRPC internally"]}}
